div.head: J Missing proofs from Section 3
div.p: This appendix contains the proofs of the main results of this work: Theorem 6 and Theorem 8. We organize the presentation of the proofs as follows. For Theorem 6, we first establish a concentration inequality for dependent data, similar in spirit to the Bretagnolle-Huber-Carol inequality, stated as Lemma 5, which is then used to prove the theorem. Similarly, for Theorem 8, we prove a concentration inequality adapted to the transductive setting (Lemma 7), which forms the basis of the proof of the main result.

div.head: J.1 Dependency graphs and concentration inequalities
div.p: We begin with the formal definition of dependency graphs, which model dependencies between random variables. Definition 26 (Dependency graphs). Let (Ω, F, P ) be a probability space and (E, E) a measurable space. Given n ∈ N and random variables X i : Ω → E, for i ∈ [n], let G be an undirected graph with

We say that G is a dependency graph of X = (X 1 , . . . , X n ), or that X is G-dependent, if for all disjoint subsets I, J ⊂ [n], whenever I and J are not adjacent in G (i.e., for all u ∈ I, v ∈ J such that {u, v} / ∈ E(G)), the collections {X i } i∈I and {X j } j∈J are independent.

This structure allows us to extend concentration inequalities designed initially for independent variables to settings where controlled dependencies exist by penalizing the complexity of the corresponding dependency graph, as shown by the following theorem. Theorem 27 (Janson [2004, Theorem 2.1]). Let (Ω, F, P ) be a probability space, and let X i : (Ω, F) → R, for i ∈ [n], be random variables with dependency graph G. Suppose that each X i takes values in a real interval of length at most c i ≥ 0 for all i ∈ [n]. Then, for every t > 0,
div.formula: P n i=1 X i -E n i=1 X i ≥ t ≤ exp -2t 2 χ(G)∥c∥ 2 2
div.p: , where c = (c 1 , . . . , c n ), and χ(G) denotes the chromatic number of G. More specifically, the chromatic number χ(G) in the bound can be replaced by the fractional chromatic number of G, which provides a tighter bound, as it lower bounds χ(G); see [Janson, 2004].

We now use Theorem 27 to prove a variant of the Bretagnolle-Huber-Carol inequality for nonindependent samples through the following lemma. Lemma 28 (Restated, Lemma 5). Let (Ω, F, P ) be a probability space, let n ∈ N, and let G ∈ G n . Suppose that X i : Ω → A ⊂ R are G-dependent random variables with identical distribution µ, for all i ∈ [n]. Furthermore, assume that there exists K ∈ N such that the set A can be partitioned into K disjoint subsets, denoted by {C j } K j=1 . Define the random variables
div.formula: Z j = n i=1 1 {Xi∈Cj } , for j ∈ [K],
div.p: where 1 denotes the indicator function. Then, for all t > 0, the following inequality holds,
div.formula: P   K j=1 |Z j -nµ(C j )| ≥ 2t   ≤ 2 K+1 exp -2t 2 χ(G)n ,
div.p: where, χ(G) denotes the chromatic number of the dependency graph G.
Proof. We first observe that
div.formula: K j=1 |Z j -nµ(C j )| = j : Zj ≥nµ(Cj ) (Z j -nµ(C j )) + j : Zj <nµ(Cj ) (nµ(C j ) -Z j ) = max S⊂[K] j∈S (Z j -nµ(C j )) A + max S⊂[K] j∈S (nµ(C j ) -Z j ) B ≤ 2 max{A, B}.
div.p: Therefore,
div.formula: P   K j=1 |Z j -nµ(C j )| ≥ 2t   ≤ P (max{A, B} ≥ t) = P ((A ≥ t) ∪ (B ≥ t)) ≤ P (A ≥ t) + P (B ≥ t).
div.p: Now, note that
div.formula: P (A ≥ t) = P   S⊂[K]    j∈S (Z j -nµ(C j )) ≥ t      ≤ S⊂[K] P   j∈S (Z j -nµ(C j )) ≥ t   = S⊂[K] P   j∈S Z j -n j∈S µ(C j ) ≥ t   . Now, observe that j∈S Z j = n i=1 Y (S) i
div.p: , where Y are also G-dependent. By Theorem 27, we obtain
div.formula: P (A ≥ t) ≤ S⊂[K] P n i=1 Y (S) i -E n i=1 Y (S) i ≥ t ≤ S⊂[K] exp -2t 2 χ(G)n = 2 K exp -2t 2 χ(G)n .
div.p: Similarly, noting that j∈S -Z
div.formula: j = n i=1 Y (S) i
div.p: , where ] = -j∈S µ(C j ), we obtain:
div.formula: Y (S) i = -j∈S 1 Xi∈Cj , Y(
P (B ≥ t) ≤ S⊂[K] P n i=1 Y (S) i -E n i=1 Y (S) i ≥ t ≤ S⊂[K] exp -2t 2 χ(G)n = 2 K exp -2t 2 χ(G)n .
div.p: Hence,
div.formula: P   K j=1 |Z j -nµ(C j )| ≥ 2t   ≤ 2 K+1 exp -2t 2 χ(G)n .
div.p: We are now ready to prove the first robustness generalization bound for the inductive setting.

Theorem 29 (Restated, Theorem 6). Let (Ω, F, P ) be a probability space, and let A be a (K, ε)uniformly robust learning algorithm on Z. Then, for every δ > 0, with probability at least 1 -δ (under P ), and for all samples S with dependency graph G[S], the following inequality holds:
div.formula: |ℓ exp (A S ) -ℓ emp (A S )| ≤ ε + M χ(G[S]) 2(K + 1) log 2 + 2 log 1 δ |S| ,
div.p: where ℓ exp (h S ) denotes the expected loss, ℓ emp (h S ) denotes the empirical loss, χ(G[S]) is the chromatic number of the dependency graph induced
Proof. Let {C j } K j=1 be the partition of Z by the robustness property, and
div.formula: N j = {s ∈ S | s ∈ C j }, for j ∈ [K], then, |ℓ exp (A S ) -ℓ emp (A S )| = K j=1 E(ℓ(A S , z) | z ∈ C j )µ(C j ) - 1 |S| s∈S ℓ(A S , s) ≤ K j=1 E(ℓ(A S , z) | z ∈ C j ) |N j | |S| - 1 |S| s∈S ℓ(A S , s) + K j=1 E(ℓ(A S , z) | z ∈ C j )µ(C j ) - K j=1 E(ℓ(A S , z) | z ∈ C j ) |N j | |S| ≤ 1 |S| K j=1 s∈Nj max z2∈Cj |ℓ(A S , s) -ℓ(A S , z 2 )| + max z∈Z |ℓ(A S , z)| K j=1 |N j | |S| -µ(C j ) ≤ ε + M K j=1 |N j | |S| -µ(C j ) ≤ ε + M χ(G[S]) 2(K + 1) log 2 + 2 log 1 δ |S| ,
div.p: where in the last inequality we applied Lemma 5.

div.head: J.2 Transductive and concentration inequalities
div.p: We now continue with the transductive setting. First, we must formally define the transductive stability property described in Section 3.

Before defining the notion of stability, we introduce the following notation. Given a permutation π ∈ S n and indices i, j ∈ [n] with i ̸ = j, we denote by π i,j the permutation s π ∈ S n obtained by swapping the elements at positions i and j in π. More precisely, s π(k) = π(k), for k ̸ = i, j, while s π(i) = π(j) and s π(j) = π(i). We now formally define the notion of stability following an adaptation of Bousquet and Elisseeff [2002]. Definition 30 (Uniform transductive stability). A transductive learning algorithm A has uniform transductive stability β > 0 if, for all π ∈ S n , i ∈ [m], and j ∈
div.formula: [n] \ [m], the following holds, max 1≤k≤n |A π (x k ) -A π i,j (x k )| ≤ β.
div.p: The following lemma shows that if a transductive learning algorithm satisfies uniform stability and the loss function is Lipschitz continuous, then the absolute difference between the expected empirical risk R m and the expected transductive risk R u is bounded by a constant that depends on the stability parameter and the Lipschitz constant. Lemma 31. Let A be a transductive learning algorithm satisfying uniform transductive stability β, for β > 0, and Y = {0, 1} or R. Then, for a loss function ℓ : H × V (G) × Y → R + that is bounded by M > 0 and satisfies the following Lipschitz property. For all π ∈ S n , x,
div.formula: x ′ ∈ V (G), y ∈ Y, |ℓ(A π , x, y) -ℓ(A π , x ′ , y)| ≤ L ℓ |A π (x) -A π (x ′ )|, we have that, |E π [R m ] -E π [R u ]| ≤ L ℓ • β.
div.p: Proof. By Pechyony [2008, Lemma 7], we have
div.formula: |E π [R m ] -E π [R u ]| = E π, i∼I m 1 , j∼I u m+1 [ℓ(A π i,j , x i , y i ) -ℓ(A π , x i , y i )]
div.p: , where i ∼ I m 1 denotes that i is sampled uniformly from {1, . . . , m}, and j ∼ I u m+1 denotes that j is sampled uniformly from {m + 1, . . . , u}.
Applying the Lipschitz continuity of the loss function ℓ (second inequality), and the uniform stability (third inequality), we obtain:
div.formula: E π, i∼I m 1 , j∼I u m+1 [ℓ(A π i,j , x i , y i ) -ℓ(A π , x i , y i )] ≤ E π, i∼I m 1 , j∼I u m+1 [|ℓ(A π i,j , x i , y i ) -ℓ(A π , x i , y i )|] ≤ L ℓ E π, i∼I m 1 , j∼I u m+1 [|A π i,j (x i ) -A π (x i )|] ≤ L ℓ E π, i∼I m 1 , j∼I u m+1 [β] = L ℓ β.
div.p: Below, we prove the concentration inequality for the transductive setting that is required to establish our main theorem. Lemma 32 (Restated, Lemma 7). Let (Ω, F, P ) be a probability space, and n, n ′ ∈ N with n < n ′ . If Z = {z 1 , z 2 , . . . , z n ′ } be an arbitary finite set, {C j } j∈[K] being a partition of Z, π ∼ Unif(S n ′ ), and
div.formula: X j = n i=1 1 {z π(i) ∈Cj } ,
div.p: then the following inequality holds. For all S ⊂ [K], and for all t > 0,
div.formula: P   j∈S X j -E π   j∈S X j   ≥ t   ≤ 2exp -t 2 2|S| 2 • n . Proof. For S ⊂ [K], let f S : S n → {0, 1} with f S (π) = j∈S n i=1 1 {z π(i) ∈Cj } .
div.p: We define the filtration F i = ∪ i j=0 σ(π(j)), for i ∈ [n] where, by convention, σ(π(0)) := {Ω, ∅} is the trivial σ-algebra.
Next, we define (W i ) n i=0 as the Doob martingale of the random variable f (π) with respect to the filtration {F
div.formula: i } n i=1 . That is, W 0 = E π (f S (π)), W i = E(f S (π)|F i ), for i ∈ [n].
div.p: We aim to apply Azuma's inequality (Theorem 19). To do so, we need to bound the differences
div.formula: |W k -W k-1 |, for k ∈ [n]. We have, |W k -W k-1 | = |E(f (π) | F k ) -E(f (π) | F k-1 )| = E   j∈S n i=1 1 {z π(i) ∈Cj } F k   -E   j∈S n i=1 1 {z π(i) ∈Cj } F k-1   = n i=k j∈S E(1 {z π(i) ∈Cj } | F k ) -E(1 {z π(i) ∈Cj } | F k-1 ) ≤ j∈S n i=k E(1 {z π(i) ∈Cj } | F k ) -E(1 {z π(i) ∈Cj } |F k-1 ) A i,j,k
div.p: , where we have used the tower property, i.e., E(X
div.formula: | F) = E(X) if X is F-measurable. We now show that A i,j,k ≤ 1 n-k , for all k ≤ n, i ∈ [n] \ [k], and j ∈ S. Let m (k) j = {i ∈ [k] | z π(i) ∈ C j } .
div.p: Since π is a uniformly random permutation, we have:
div.formula: E π (1 {z π(i) ∈Cj } |F k-1 ) = |C j | -m (k-1) j n -(k -1) ,and

E π (1 {z π(i) ∈Cj } |F k ) = |C j | -m (k) j n -k = |C j | -m (k-1) j -1 {z π(k) ∈Cj } n -k . If 1 {z π(k) ∈Cj } = 1, we set a = |C j | + m (k-1) j -1 and b = n -k, obtaining A i,j,k = a b - a + 1 b + 1 = 1 b + 1 b -a b ≤ 1 b . Similarly, if 1 {z π(k) ∈Cj } = 0, setting a = |C j | + m (k-1) j and b = n -k gives A i,j,k = a b - a b + 1 = 1 b + 1 b -a b ≤ 1 (b + 1) ≤ 1 b . Therefore, we conclude that A i,j,k ≤ 1 n-k , which implies |W k -W k-1 | ≤ |S|.
div.p: Applying Azuma's inequality, we obtain
div.formula: P (|f S (π) -E π (f S (π))| ≥ t) = P   j∈S X j -E π   j∈S X j   ≥ t   ≤ 2 exp -t 2 2n|S| 2 .
div.p: Finally, we restate and prove the main robustness generalization theorem for the transductive setting. Theorem 33 (Restated, Theorem 8). Let (Ω, F, P ) be a probability space, ℓ be a loss function bounded by M satisfying the conditions of Lemma 31. If A is a transductive learning algorithm on Z = {z i } m+u i=1 with hypothesis class H that is (K, ε)-uniformly-robust and satisfies uniform transductive stability β > 0. Then, for every δ > 0, with probability at least 1 -δ, the following inequality holds,
div.formula: 1 m m i=1 ℓ(A π , x π(i) , y π(i) ) - 1 u m+u i=m+1 ℓ(A π , x π(i) , y π(i) ) ≤ 2ε + 1 √ m + 1 √ u • M • K • 2(K + 1) log 2 + 2 log 1 δ + L ℓ β,
div.p: where M is an upper bound for the loss function ℓ.
Proof. We begin by considering the absolute difference:
div.formula: |R m -R u | ≤ |R m -E π [R m ]| + |R u -E π [R u ]| + |E π [R m ] -E π [R u ]| = |R m -E π [ℓ(A π , x π(1) , y π(1) )]| + |R u -E π [ℓ(A π , x π(m+1) , y π(m+1) )]| + |E π [R m ] -E π [R u ]| ≤ |R m -E π [ℓ(A π , x π(1) , y π(1) )]| + |R u -E π [ℓ(A π , x π(m+1) , y π(m+1) )]| + L ℓ β.
div.p: The first inequality follows from the triangle inequality. The equality follows from the symmetry of the learning algorithm (Remark 3). The last inequality is obtained by applying Lemma 31.
Next, we proceed similarly to the proof of Theorem 6, but we employ Lemma 7 instead of Lemma 5 to bound the term |R m -E π [ℓ(A π , x π(1) , y π( 1) )]|. Using the same reasoning, we can bound
div.formula: |R u - E π [ℓ(A π , x π(m+1) , y π(m+1) )]|. Defining N m j = {i ∈ [m] | z π(i) ∈ C j }, we obtain, |R m -E π [ℓ(A π , x π(1) , y π(1) )]| = K j=1 E π (ℓ(A π , z π(1) ) | z π(1) ∈ C j )P (z π(1) ∈ C j ) - 1 m m i=1 ℓ(A π , z π(i) ) ≤ K j=1 E π (ℓ(A π , z π(1) ) | z π(1) ∈ C j ) |N m j | m - 1 m m i=1 ℓ(A π , z π(i) ) + K j=1 E π (ℓ(A π , z π(1) ) | z π(1) ∈ C j )P (z π(1) ∈ C j ) - K j=1 E π ℓ(A π , z π(1) ) | z π(1) ∈ C j |N m j | m ≤ 1 m K j=1 z∈N m j max z ′ ∈Cj |ℓ(A π , z) -ℓ(A π , z ′ )| + max z∈Z |ℓ(A π , z)| K j=1 |N m j | m -P (z π(1) ∈ C j ) ≤ ε + M K j=1 |N m j | m - |C j | n .
div.p: Finally, for t ≥ we apply Lemma 7 to bound the probability,
div.formula: P   K j=1 |N m j | m - |C j | n ≥ t   .
div.p: Following the derivations from the proof of Lemma 5, we obtain
div.formula: P   K j=1 |N m j | m - |C j | n ≥ t   ≤ S⊂[K] P m i=1 Y (S) i -E m i=1 Y (S) i ≥ mt 2 , where Y (S) i = j∈S 1 {z π(i) ∈Cj } .
div.p: Applying Lemma 7, we obtain
div.formula: P   K j=1 |N m j | m - |C j | n ≥ t   ≤ S⊂[K] 2 exp -t 2 m 2|S| 2 ≤ 2 K+1 exp -t 2 m 2K 2 .
div.p: Thus, for any δ > 0, with probability at least 1 -δ, we have
div.formula: K j=1 |N m j | m - |C j | n ≤ 1 √ m • K • 2(K + 1) log 2 + 2 log 1 δ .
div.p: Following the same reasoning for
div.formula: |R u -E π (R u )|, we derive, |R m -R u | ≤ 2ε + 1 √ m + 1 √ u • M • K • 2(K + 1) log 2 + 2 log 1 δ + L ℓ β.

div.head: K Missing proofs from Section 4
div.p: The main theorems in this section are direct consequences of results established earlier. Specifically, Theorem 9 follows immediately by combining Theorem 6 with Theorem 3, and Theorem 10 by combining Theorem 8 with Theorem 3.
Specifically, the Lipschitz constant C appearing in Theorem 9 and Theorem 10 is the same and is given by
div.formula: C = 2 LL ℓ L t=1 L φt ,
div.p: where L φt denotes the Lipschitz constant of the t-th message-passing layer as defined in Equation (2), L ℓ the Lipschitz constant of the loss function, and L is the Lipschitz constant established in Proposition 25.

On assumptions in node prediction bounds Previous works such as Scarselli et al. [2018], Verma and Zhang [2019], Garg et al. [2020] study generalization in node prediction under an inductive setting, but rely on strong and limiting assumptions. Specifically, they assume that graphs decompose into independent substructures, similarly to the computational trees we defined in Appendix G, and that the data distribution is defined over the product space of these trees and their labels. A more restrictive assumption is that these tree-label pairs are sampled independently, even when the trees originate from the same graph, thus ignoring inherent dependencies within the graph structure. In contrast, our framework treats each graph as a relational object, without reducing it to a collection of unrollings. While we relax the i.i.d. assumption, we still capture dependencies within the training set through a mild and natural condition: independence is assumed only across nodes from different graphs. In contrast, dependencies are allowed among nodes within the same graph. This setting better reflects real-world scenarios and provides more realistic assumptions than prior work.
Finally, we state and prove the following corollary, showing that if we restrict our attention to graphs with maximum node degree q, for some q ∈ N, we can bound the covering number from Theorem 9.
Corollary 34. Let G (-1,1) d,q
be the space of featured graphs with node features in (-1, 1) d and maximum node degree q, and consider the setting from Theorem 9. Then, , for all ε > 0.

Proof. The proof is a straightforward application of the bound on the covering number of a ddimensional unit Euclidean ball with radius 0 < ϵ < 1, which is at most 3 ϵ d (see Handel [2014, Lemma 5.13]).

div.head: L Experimental analysis
div.p: This appendix presents the experimental protocol underlying the results and insights discussed in Section 5. The source code of all methods is available in the supplementary material.

Datasets To investigate Q1 we focus on the transductive setting for node predictions, and we use the datasets Wisconsin, Cornell, Texas [Pei et al., 2020] available as part of the WebKB dataset available at https://github.com/bingzhewei/geom-gcn and Cora dataset [Yang et al., 2016, Sen et al., 2008] available under the CC-BY 4.0 license at https://pytorch-geometric.readthedocs. io/en/latest/generated/torch_geometric.datasets.Planetoid.html. To investigate Q2 under the inductive setting, we consider Pattern, and Cluster datasets [Dwivedi et al., 2023]. These datasets are available under the MIT license at https://github.com/graphdeeplearning/ benchmarking-gnns.

We compute the covering number for all transductive datasets. In the case of the inductive datasets, we omit the covering number computation due to its similarity to the computations provided by [Vasileiou et al., 2024a]. Common dataset statistics and properties are in Table 2. Neural architectures To address Q1, we use randomly initialized GIN and SEAL architectures with 3 layers. We further incorporate node features into the GIN architecture as initial inputs to the model. Nonlinearity is introduced via the ReLU function as discussed in our theoretical examination.

A complete list of hyperparameters used can be found in Table 6. Note that the slope of the line that upper bounds the observations in Figure 3 and Figure 5 can be used as an upper bound on the Lipschitz constant C in Theorem 10.

In the case of SEAL, we also use GIN as the underlying GNN architecture. We sample one negative link for each link in the dataset, providing an equal amount of positive and negative links. Across all datasets, we use the commonly used data splits, and for SEAL, an 80/10/10 train-valid-test split.

Like the node prediction tasks, we use ReLU nonlinearity and sum pooling to compute each link representation. The subgraph sampling is done as proposed in the original SEAL paper [Zhang and Chen, 2018], omitting the target link in its respective subgraph.

Hyperparameters For inductive node classification on Pattern and Cluster, we trained for 200 epochs using a modified version of GIN, aligning with Equation (2). In addition, we tuned the learning rate using the set {0.01, 0.001, 0.0001}, while using the Adam optimizer [Kingma and Ba, 2015]. Across all tasks and models, we used a batch size of 32 and set dropout to 0.1. Further, we do not use learning rate decay across all datasets. We report results on the inductive node classification tasks and the sampling strategies in Table 1. A list of all hyperparameters used can be found in Table 6 and Table 3 for each experiment.

For the correlation experiments, we use a randomly initialized GIN model and a SEAL model using GIN layers. We use 3 layers each to align the computation with possible computations of the generalization bounds. We set the hidden dimension to 16 for Cornell, Texas, and Wisconsin and 32 for Cora, respectively. Since we do not train the models, we omit further training-specific hyperparameters.

Further, we report our experiments' runtime and memory usage in Table 5. We provide a Pytorch Geometric implementation for each model. All our experiments were executed on a system with 12 CPU cores, an Nvidia L40 GPU, and 120GB of memory.

Experimental protocol and model configuration To evaluate Q1, we measure whether the perturbed inputs to the generalized distance lead to perturbations in the MPNN outputs. We use the same 80/10/10 train-valid-test split as for the other tasks. We then select nodes or links randomly from the transductive dataset for each dataset. We then compute the (unrolling-based) distances for two sampled nodes or links and compare them to the Euclidean distance of their respective GIN or SEAL outputs. Sample plots for selected nodes showing the correlation between the generalized distance and MPNN outputs are shown in Figure 3, Figure 4, Figure 5, and Figure 6.

Concerning Q2, we provide two scenarios for sampling nodes from the graphs in the dataset. First, we consider the case of the train dataset nodes to be sampled from specific graphs. Secondly, we assume uniform node subsampling across all training graphs. We fix the test set for these experiments to a randomly determined train-valid-test split. We report the obtained results in Table 1, which showcase the difference in generalization performance between each sampling method. Node samples denote the fraction of nodes used for the computation.

Sampling strategies Below, we describe the two sampling strategies used to construct the experiments in Table 1. The goal was to train a GIN-based MPNN for node prediction in the inductive setting, using multiple graphs and two training sets of equal size.

all nodes in a path of length at most k from v. When k = 1, we refer to elements of N (1) G (v) as neighbors of v and we omit k in the notation. The degree of a node v is |N G (v)|. When referring to a directed graph, we use the notation N

and define initial vertex features using the INIT function such that INIT(u, u) ̸ = INIT(u, v) for all u ̸ = v. The following condition defines the edge relation in G ′ , {(u, v), (x, y)} ∈ E(G ′ ) ⇐⇒ u = x and y ∈ N G (v). The transformation and selection functions are then defined as: T (G) = G ′ , and V(G, (u, v)) = {(u, w) | w ∈ N G (v)}. Again, we use sum-pooling as our pooling function Ψ . By the definition of C-MPNNs, we can slightly modify the transformation and selection function to capture the NBFNets.

figure.head: Table 1 :
figure.figDesc: Generalization results for different sampling strategies related to Q2. Strategy (1) refers to training nodes sampled from a few graphs (resulting in higher sample dependency), while strategy (2) uses nodes sampled uniformly across many distinct graphs. Both strategies use the same number of training and test nodes. D S denotes the number of distinct graphs in the training set. Further, n train and n test denote the number of train and test nodes. initial step in unraveling how graph structure influences the generalization abilities of MPNNs in node-and link-level prediction tasks. Beyond this, it provides a broad and flexible framework for analyzing generalization in data-dependent settings, guided by the structural properties of the input space through appropriately defined pseudo-metrics. figure.figDesc: . Most recently, building on the robustness framework ofXu and Mannor [2012],Vasileiou et al. [2024a] derived MPNNs' generalization abilities for graph-level predictions by studying different pseudo-metrics capturing MPNNs' computation, improving over the results inMorris et al. [2023]. Finally,Yehudai et al. [2021] showed negative results for MPNNs' generalization ability to larger graphs.SeeVasileiou et al. [2024b] for a survey on generalization analyses of MPNNs and related architectures.

note.p: Observe that computing a metric on the set of graphs G up to isomorphism is at least as hard as solving the graph isomorphism problem on G.

Strictly speaking, the 1-WL and color refinement are two different algorithms. That is, the 1-WL considers neighbors and non-neighbors to update the coloring, resulting in a slightly higher expressive power when distinguishing nodes in a given graph; seeGrohe [2021] for details. Following customs in the machine learning literature, we consider both algorithms equivalent.
Here, we implicitly assume an injective function from Σ to N.

In this notation, without loss of generality and for simplicity, we assume that |NF 1 (x)| ≥ |NF 2 (y)|. If this assumption does not hold, the notation can be trivially adjusted to reflect the reverse case.
